name: Erymanthian Boar
description: The Erymanthian Boar was a monstrous wild boar that inhabited the area around Mount Erymanthos in Arcadia, Greece. It was renowned for its immense size, ferocious strength, and savage nature. The boar was a terror to the local population, destroying crops, killing livestock, and generally wreaking havoc. Its tusks were incredibly sharp and capable of tearing through flesh and bone with ease. The capture of the Erymanthian Boar was the fourth labor assigned to Heracles (Hercules) by King Eurystheus of Tiryns. Eurystheus hoped that the task would prove fatal, but Heracles, with his unparalleled strength and cunning, managed to overcome the beast. The labor symbolizes overcoming primal, destructive forces and bringing order to chaos. The boar's capture demonstrated Heracles' ability to control even the most savage creatures, making the world safer for humankind. The pursuit of the boar led Heracles to various encounters, including a stay with the centaur Pholus, which ultimately led to a tragic incident involving poisoned arrows.
subtitle: A savage beast, tamed by Heracles
appearance: The Erymanthian Boar was a massive, fearsome boar with thick, bristly fur, sharp tusks protruding from its mouth, and glowing red eyes. Its size was significantly larger than that of a normal boar, and its powerful build suggested immense strength. Its hide was thick and tough, providing natural armor against most attacks.
habitat: Mount Erymanthos, Arcadia, Greece; dense forests and rugged terrain.
behavior: The Erymanthian Boar was an extremely aggressive and territorial creature. It was known for its destructive rampages, tearing up fields, attacking livestock, and posing a constant threat to the local population. It was solitary and fiercely protective of its domain.
weaknesses: Susceptible to exhaustion after prolonged chase

keyMyths.title: The Fourth Labor of Heracles
keyMyths.description: Heracles was tasked with capturing the Erymanthian Boar alive as his fourth labor. He chased the boar into deep snow, tiring it out before trapping it and carrying it back to Eurystheus.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive account of Greek mythology.
sources.text: A comprehensive account of Greek mythology.
